facts.title: Hangul was invented, not evolved
facts.fact: Korea's alphabet, Hangul, was created in 1443 under King Sejong the Great. It is one of the few writing systems with a known inventor and creation date, and its letter shapes mirror the mouth and tongue positions used to make each sound.
facts.note: **한글날** (Hangul Day) is a national holiday on **October 9** that celebrates the alphabet.

facts.title: The Three Kingdoms
facts.fact: For centuries the peninsula was divided among three kingdoms — Goguryeo, Baekje, and Silla — until Silla unified most of it in 668.
facts.korean_en: Long ago there were three kingdoms in Korea.
facts.note: 고구려, 백제, 신라 are the **삼국** (Three Kingdoms).

facts.title: Seoul, a 600-year capital
facts.fact: When the Joseon dynasty made Seoul its capital in 1394, it began a run of more than 600 years as Korea's center of power that continues today.
facts.korean_en: Seoul is the capital of Korea.
facts.note: 한국의 means "Korea's" — the particle **의** marks possession.

facts.title: Gojoseon and the Dangun myth
facts.fact: According to legend, **Dangun** founded **Gojoseon**, the first Korean kingdom, in 2333 BCE. The myth tells of a bear that became a woman and gave birth to him, making Dangun the grandson of the sky god.
facts.note: **개천절** (National Foundation Day), October 3, celebrates the founding of Gojoseon.

facts.title: Goryeo celadon
facts.fact: The **Goryeo** dynasty produced jade-green **celadon** pottery so refined that Chinese writers praised its color as "first under heaven." Its delicate inlay technique remains a high point of East Asian ceramics.

facts.title: The Tripitaka Koreana
facts.fact: The **Tripitaka Koreana** is a complete set of Buddhist scriptures carved onto more than 80,000 woodblocks in the 13th century. Stored at **Haeinsa** temple, the blocks have survived almost 800 years without warping or decay.
facts.korean_en: The Tripitaka Koreana was made from wood.
facts.note: Koreans call it **팔만대장경**, literally the "80,000 Tripitaka," after the number of woodblocks.

facts.title: Jikji, the oldest metal-type book
facts.fact: **Jikji**, a Buddhist text printed in Korea in **1377**, is the world's oldest surviving book made with movable metal type — about 78 years before Gutenberg's Bible. One volume is kept today at the National Library of France.
facts.korean_en: Jikji was made with metal type.
facts.note: **금속** means "metal"; the **으로** particle here marks the material something is made of.

facts.title: Admiral Yi Sun-sin
facts.fact: Admiral **Yi Sun-sin** defended Korea during the Japanese invasions of the 1590s and is famous for never losing a naval battle. At **Myeongnyang** in 1597 his small fleet defeated a far larger enemy force.
facts.note: His statue stands in **광화문** (Gwanghwamun) square in central Seoul.

facts.title: The turtle ship
facts.fact: The **geobukseon**, or "turtle ship," was an armored warship used by Admiral Yi Sun-sin. Its covered deck, studded with iron spikes, protected the crew while a dragon-head prow let it fire cannons forward.
facts.korean_en: The turtle ship resembles a turtle.

facts.title: Silla gold crowns
facts.fact: The kingdom of **Silla** was so rich in gold that its rulers wore elaborate golden crowns shaped like trees and antlers. Several survive today and are national treasures, hinting at Silla's wealth and trade ties across Asia.
facts.korean_en: The Silla king wore a gold crown.
facts.note: The verb **쓰다** is used for wearing things on the **head**, like hats and crowns.

facts.title: Hanji, traditional Korean paper
facts.fact: **Hanji** is handmade paper produced from the bark of the mulberry tree. Famous for its strength and long life, it was used for books, windows, and even clothing, and good hanji can last over a thousand years.
facts.note: **한지** means "Korean paper"; the **지** here is the Sino-Korean word for paper.

facts.title: The Japanese occupation
facts.fact: From **1910 to 1945** Korea was a colony of Japan. During this time use of the Korean language was suppressed, yet independence movements kept the national identity alive.
facts.note: **광복절** (Liberation Day), August 15, marks the end of the occupation in 1945.

facts.title: The March First Movement
facts.fact: On **March 1, 1919**, Koreans held nationwide peaceful demonstrations to demand independence from Japan. Crowds read a declaration of independence and waved the **Taegukgi**, the Korean flag, in the streets.
facts.korean_en: The people held up the Korean flag.
facts.note: **삼일절** (March First Day) is a national holiday remembering the 1919 movement.

facts.title: The Korean War
facts.fact: The **Korean War** lasted from **1950 to 1953** and ended in an armistice rather than a peace treaty. The fighting divided the peninsula into North and South along a line near the 38th parallel.
facts.note: Because no peace treaty was signed, the two Koreas are technically still at war today.

facts.title: The DMZ
facts.fact: After the Korean War, a **Demilitarized Zone (DMZ)** about 250 km long was created between the two Koreas. Because people rarely enter it, the strip has unexpectedly become a refuge for rare wildlife.
facts.korean_en: There is a demilitarized zone between South and North Korea.
facts.note: **사이** means "between"; the particle **에** marks the location where something exists.

facts.title: The democratization movement
facts.fact: South Korea was ruled by military governments for decades, but large protests in **June 1987** forced the country to adopt direct presidential elections. This peaceful turn helped build today's democracy.
facts.note: The 1987 protests are often called the **6월 민주 항쟁** (June Democratic Struggle).

facts.title: The Gwangju Uprising
facts.fact: In **May 1980**, citizens of the city of **Gwangju** rose up to demand democracy and resisted military force for several days. The uprising became a powerful symbol of South Korea's long struggle for freedom.
facts.korean_en: The people of Gwangju fought for democracy.
facts.note: **을/를 위해** means "for the sake of" something — here, fighting **for** democracy.

facts.title: The 1988 Seoul Olympics
facts.fact: Seoul hosted the **Summer Olympics in 1988**, the first held in South Korea. The games introduced the country to a global audience and showcased its rapid economic rise after the war.
facts.korean_en: In 1988 the Olympics were held in Seoul.
facts.note: The particle **에서** marks **where** an action happens, so 서울에서 means "in Seoul."

facts.title: Gyeongju, the museum without walls
facts.fact: **Gyeongju** was the capital of the Silla kingdom for nearly a thousand years. So many temples, tombs, and pagodas remain there that it is often called "a museum without walls."
facts.korean_en: Gyeongju was the capital of Silla.
facts.note: **였습니다** is the past tense of **이다** ("to be") after a vowel — "was."
